text: Makes sense. It's one of those films where the experience of watching it in a cinema — proper screen, proper sound — is almost impossible to replicate at home, and yet it's also one of the most rewatched films precisely because people are chasing that feeling again. The pacing in the second act is what I think divides people most. It slows down significantly when it shifts focus, and if you're not fully bought in emotionally by that point, it probably loses you. It's also one of the few big-budget films where the science is treated as genuinely interesting rather than just window dressing.
label: critique

text: I can't stand Andrew Scott, especially in Sherlock. He was insufferable.
label: hot_take

text: 2001 is my answer to this thread. But Jesus Christ it's aged UNBELIEVABLY well. It does not look like a late 60s movie at all.
label: hot_take

text: Once Upon A Time In Hollywood — I for the most part LOVE Tarantino's movies, but this was NOT good imo. Maybe I just didn't get it? I have no clue, but it was blegh.
label: hot_take

text: I don't have any suggestions because I'm pretty new to the horror genre, but I just wanted to say Inde Navarette made Obsession good. At its base, it's a pretty common concept, but her acting was so phenomenal in it that it really elevated it to a whole other level.
label: critique

text: Manchester by the sea. It's a specific type of sadness though. More of a slow burn melancholy.
label: reaction

text: The 'I can't beat it.' scene. I lost it. One of my favorite films of all time and lead performances.
label: reaction

text: LaKeith Stanfield — I haven't loved everything he's been in, but I've always found him very watchable.
label: hot_take

text: City of God. I couldn't play cod for like 2 weeks after watching it. Laugh at me all you want but that movie hit me like a damn freight train.
label: reaction

text: Late Night With The Devil is super underrated imo. Not in the leagues of these 3, rosemary, exorcist etc. But very well made.
label: hot_take

text: I felt like I needed a shower after I watched Threads. Just sapped all of the happiness out of my life for a solid day.
label: reaction

text: Emilia Perez: it's a musical where all the songs are bad for starters. I don't think Gomez is necessarily bad either — the script she was given was dreadful. It's like all the good writing was left for Saldana and the rest of the cast were left to feed on scraps.
label: critique

text: It makes sense. It's an accessible and well-made sci-fi film from a pseudo-intellectual director. For people getting into film, it's a good entry point that bridges them to greater works.
label: critique

text: The new Frankenstein adaptation literally has a character say to Victor 'YOU'RE the real monster!'
label: hot_take

text: It gets better every time you rewatch it — there are very few movies that actually do that.
label: hot_take

text: Wolf Man (2025) would have been so much better if the Generational Trauma wasn't 'being told off for misbehaving'. Still wouldn't have been good, but it would be better.
label: critique

text: I think how much you like Once Upon a Time in Hollywood is determined by your threshold for movies where a bunch of good actors essentially just vibe for an hour or two.
label: critique

text: I realized a while ago that I just love old Hollywood and don't really click with arthouse cinema. But it took a lot of wasted hours trying to make it through Godard movies that LOOKED cool but did nothing for me.
label: reaction

text: Watching it in a cinema was a fun experience. Watching at home I actually found it really embarrassing to watch at times. Really tumblr-style humor hammered into the viewer.
label: critique

text: it's one of those movies that you may not immediately think of to rewatch, but once on, you will find yourself standing in front of the tv for longer than you should've
label: reaction

text: Past Lives, not during the pivotal scene, but for some reason when Hae-Sung was looking out of his taxi at the end
label: reaction

text: It getting a cinematography nom is also a joke. I feel like people confused production design with cinematography. The sets are beautiful, stunning things. But if only they'd been filmed with something other than the same shitty wide-angle lens and handheld camera with bland Netflix lighting the entire time, we might have been able to actually appreciate how amazing it all could have looked.
label: critique

text: Mysterious Skin (2004) — great movie, absolutely heartbreaking.
label: reaction
